originPoint.name: Mount Sinai
originPoint.description: Given to Moses at Mount Sinai, later housed in the Ark of the Covenant in Jerusalem's Temple

mythologyContexts.culturalSignificance: The Tablets are the supreme symbol of Jewish law and ethics. They appear atop Torah scrolls in synagogues worldwide, are depicted in Jewish art and architecture, and represent Judaism in interfaith contexts. The tradition of keeping both broken and whole tablets has inspired countless teachings about honoring those who are 'broken' or fallen, and about the possibility of renewal. The Ten Commandments themselves form the ethical foundation not only of Judaism but of Western civilization.

rituals.description: The revelation at Mount Sinai when God gave Moses the Tablets along with the entire Torah
rituals.name: Giving of the Torah (Matan Torah)

rituals.description: Moses shattered the first tablets upon witnessing the golden calf idolatry, demonstrating righteous anger and the seriousness of covenant violation
rituals.name: Breaking of the First Tablets

rituals.description: Commemorates the giving of the Torah and Tablets at Mount Sinai
rituals.name: Shavuot Festival

mythologyContexts.usage: The foundational legal and moral code of Judaism, representing the direct revelation of God's will to Israel through Moses. The Tablets symbolize the covenant relationship and serve as the core of Torah law.
mythologyContexts.symbolism: The Tablets represent the direct communication between God and humanity, the foundation of divine law, and the covenant relationship between God and Israel. The stone material symbolizes permanence and immutability of God's law. The breaking of the first tablets demonstrates that even the most sacred objects are secondary to moral behavior - when Israel sinned with the golden calf, the tablets themselves were destroyed. The preservation of both broken and whole tablets in the Ark teaches that even broken spiritual vessels retain holiness and should be honored.

images.caption: Traditional depiction of the Tablets bearing the Aseret HaDibrot

extendedContent.title: Creation and Forging on Mount Sinai
extendedContent.content: The creation of the Tablets of the Law is intrinsically linked to the theophany at Mount Sinai, a pivotal event in Jewish history. Exodus 24:12 states, "And the Lord said to Moses, Come up to Me on the mountain and remain there, and I will give you the tablets of stone with the law and the commandments which I have written, that you may teach them." This verse establishes God as the author and giver of the Tablets. The Midrash elaborates, suggesting that the Tablets were not merely inscribed but were hewn from sapphire, a stone associated with divine glory and wisdom. Some interpretations posit that the sapphire was sourced from the Divine Throne itself, emphasizing the Tablets' celestial origin. The act of God's own 'finger' engraving the commandments signifies the direct divine authorship and the permanence of the covenant established between God and the Israelites. The Rabbis teach that the letters themselves were miraculously cut entirely through the stone, visible from both sides and defying natural laws, showcasing the supernatural nature of their creation. The process underscores the divine initiative and the extraordinary character of the revelation.

extendedContent.title: Powers and Properties: More than Stone
extendedContent.content: Beyond their physical form as inscribed sapphire tablets, the Tablets of the Law possess profound spiritual power. They are not merely a record of commandments but a conduit for divine presence and a physical manifestation of the covenant. The Talmud describes instances where the Tablets exhibited miraculous properties, such as the letters remaining intact even when the Tablets were broken. Furthermore, the Ark of the Covenant, designed to house the Tablets, was believed to possess immense power, demonstrated in stories of its use in warfare (Joshua 6:4-20) and its protection of the Israelites. The Tablets are also linked to the concept of Torah study. Jewish tradition emphasizes that studying the commandments inscribed on the Tablets brings one closer to God. The physical presence of the Tablets in the Ark served as a constant reminder of the divine law and the obligation to observe it. The destruction of the First Temple and the subsequent loss of the Ark and the Tablets are viewed as a catastrophic spiritual blow, symbolizing a broken connection with the divine.

extendedContent.title: Moses: The Conduit of the Tablets
extendedContent.content: Moses plays the central role in receiving and transmitting the Tablets of the Law. He ascended Mount Sinai to commune with God, enduring forty days and forty nights of fasting and prayer, a period of intense spiritual preparation. Upon descending, Moses discovered the Israelites worshipping the Golden Calf, a blatant violation of the very first commandment he was carrying. The sight of this idolatry filled him with righteous anger, leading him to shatter the Tablets (Exodus 32:19). This act, while seemingly destructive, is interpreted as a symbolic breaking of the covenant due to the Israelites' infidelity. God subsequently commanded Moses to hew a second set of Tablets (Exodus 34:1), which Moses then brought back up Mount Sinai. This second set, although carved by Moses, was still inscribed by God. Moses' role as the intermediary underscores his unique position as the prophet closest to God, entrusted with communicating the divine law to the people. The story highlights the tension between divine revelation and human fallibility, and the importance of upholding the covenant.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism: Covenant, Law, and Divine Authority
extendedContent.content: The Tablets of the Law are rich in symbolism, representing the core tenets of Judaism. They symbolize the covenant between God and the Israelites, a binding agreement based on mutual obligations. The ten commandments, inscribed on the Tablets, provide the foundational ethical and moral framework for Jewish life. The fact that there are two Tablets is often interpreted as representing the dual aspects of the commandments: obligations towards God (the first five) and obligations towards fellow humans (the second five). The Tablets also symbolize divine authority. They are a tangible reminder of God's presence and the binding nature of divine law. The Ark of the Covenant, which housed the Tablets, became the holiest object in the Tabernacle and later in the Temple, representing the dwelling place of God's presence among the Israelites. The loss of the Tablets and the Ark is thus seen as a symbol of the loss of divine favor and the destruction of the covenant relationship. The enduring significance of the Tablets lies in their representation of the eternal bond between God and the Jewish people and the ongoing obligation to live according to divine law.

linguistic.etymology: לֻחוֹת (luchot) plural of לוּחַ (luach) meaning 'tablet, board, plank'; הַבְּרִית (habrit) meaning 'the covenant'. Also called לֻחוֹת הָעֵדֻת (Luchot HaEidut, 'Tablets of the Testimony') and עֲשֶׂרֶת הַדִּבְּרוֹת (Aseret HaDibrot, 'Ten Utterances').

longDescription: The Tablets of the Law (Hebrew: לוחות הברית Luchot HaBrit - Tablets of the Covenant) are the two stone tablets upon which God inscribed the Ten Commandments (Aseret HaDibrot) with His own finger. Given to Moses on Mount Sinai after forty days and nights, these tablets represent the covenant between God and Israel. The first set was shattered by Moses when he witnessed the Israelites worshiping the golden calf. God then commanded Moses to hew two new tablets, upon which the commandments were re-inscribed. According to tradition, both the broken fragments and the intact second tablets were kept in the Ark of the Covenant.

description: Two stone tablets inscribed with the Ten Commandments by the finger of God, given to Moses at Mount Sinai

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Represent divine law and order, embodying principles of justice and righteousness, though not explicitly inscribed tablets.
Represent foundational principles of civilization and divine decrees, governing various aspects of Sumerian society and bestowed upon deities.
Symbolizes truth, justice, and cosmic order, representing the divine standard against which souls are judged, similar to the Law's moral code.
Inscribed with divine knowledge and power, used for divination and shaping destiny, though not explicitly commandments, they represent a codified system of understanding the world.

associations.name: Stone
associations.significance: Represents the enduring and immutable nature of God's law.

associations.name: Ten
associations.significance: Corresponds to the Ten Commandments, the core principles inscribed on the Tablets.

associations.name: Mount Sinai
associations.significance: The Tablets were received on Mount Sinai, connecting them to divine revelation.

associations.name: Authority
associations.significance: The Tablets symbolize God's ultimate authority and the foundation of Jewish law.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: Represents purity and the divine origin of the commandments.

associations.name: Covenant
associations.significance: The Tablets embody the covenant between God and the Israelites.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: The tablets illuminate the path of righteousness, guiding people towards moral living.

companions.name: Moses
companions.description: Moses received the Tablets from God and brought them down from Mount Sinai to the Israelites. He is the primary human figure associated with the Tablets.

companions.name: Joshua
companions.description: Joshua accompanied Moses up Mount Sinai and was present during the giving of the Law. He was a loyal servant to Moses and the covenant.

companions.name: Ark of the Covenant
companions.description: The Ark was built to house the Tablets of the Law, serving as their sacred and physical protector. It also represented God's presence among the Israelites.

companions.name: The Levites
companions.description: The tribe of Levi was chosen to safeguard the Ark and its contents, including the Tablets. They were responsible for its transport and protection.

companions.name: Aaron
companions.description: Aaron was Moses' brother and served as his spokesperson. He helped Moses lead the Israelites and explain the law.
